section.title: An Approved Workman
section.sectionRationale: Paul turns from endurance to the handling of the word itself. Timothy is to be a workman approved by God, accurately cutting a straight path through the word of truth, over against those whose empty talk spreads like gangrene and whose false teaching upends the faith of some. God's firm foundation still stands, and in a great house there are vessels for honor and for dishonor, so Timothy must cleanse himself, flee youthful passions, and correct opponents with gentleness that leaves room for repentance.
section.thesis: The Lord's approved workman rightly handles the word of truth and refuses corrosive controversy, resting on God's unshakable foundation, cleansing himself to be a vessel fit for honorable use, and gently correcting opponents in hope that God will grant them repentance and rescue from the devil's snare.
section.frameNote: Four movements: the approved workman who rightly handles the word against ruinous word-battles; empty chatter that spreads like gangrene, and God's firm foundation that stands; the great house and its vessels for honor and dishonor; and the Lord's servant who corrects opponents gently, hoping for their repentance.

verses.text: Remind the believers of these things, charging them before God to avoid quarreling over words, which succeeds only in leading the listeners to ruin.
Make every effort to present yourself {{dokimos|approved}} to God, an unashamed workman who {{orthotomeo|accurately handles}} the word of truth.

units.label: Gangrene and a firm foundation
units.note: error spreads, but God's seal stands

verses.text: But avoid irreverent, empty chatter, which will only lead to more ungodliness,
and the talk of such men will spread like {{gangraina|gangrene}}. Among them are Hymenaeus and Philetus,
who have deviated from the truth. They say that the resurrection has already occurred, and they undermine the faith of some.
Nevertheless, God's firm foundation stands, bearing this seal: “The Lord knows those who are His,” and, “Everyone who calls on the name of the Lord must turn away from iniquity.”

units.label: Vessels for honor
units.note: cleansed and useful to the Master

verses.text: A large house contains not only vessels of gold and silver, but also of wood and clay. Some indeed are for honorable use, but others are for common use.
So if anyone cleanses himself of what is unfit, he will be a {{skeuos|vessel}} for honor: sanctified, useful to the Master, and prepared for every good work.
Flee from youthful passions and pursue righteousness, faith, love, and peace, together with those who call on the Lord out of a pure heart.
But reject foolish and ignorant speculation, for you know that it breeds quarreling.
And a {{doulos|servant}} of the Lord must not be quarrelsome, but must be kind to everyone, able to teach, and forbearing.
He must gently reprove those who oppose him, in the hope that God may grant them repentance leading to a knowledge of the truth.
Then they will come to their senses and escape the snare of the devil, who has taken them captive to his will.

dokimos.gloss: Approved after testing, shown to be genuine, like metal proved by fire or a coin found to be true. Timothy is to present himself approved to God, seeking God's verdict rather than human applause. The word implies scrutiny passed, and it stands in pointed contrast to the false teachers who in chapter 3 are called adokimos, disqualified.

orthotomeo.gloss: Literally to cut straight, to make a straight path or cut a straight furrow. Applied to Scripture it means to handle the word accurately, keeping a straight course through it without twisting or veering. The approved workman drives a straight road through the word of truth, rightly dividing and applying it rather than distorting it for empty debate.

gangraina.gloss: Gangrene, the medical term for tissue that rots and spreads, killing what it touches. Paul uses it for false teaching: empty, godless chatter is not harmless talk but a spreading infection that consumes faith. The vivid image explains the urgency of avoiding it and cutting it off before it eats through the body of the church.

skeuos.gloss: A vessel, container, or instrument, an item of household use. In a large house some vessels are for honorable use and some for common tasks. Paul applies the picture to people: whoever cleanses himself from what is unfit becomes a vessel for honor, sanctified and useful to the Master, ready for every good work.

doulos.gloss: A slave or bondservant, one wholly owned by and devoted to a master. The Lord's servant is defined not by cleverness in argument but by character: not quarrelsome but kind, able to teach, patient, and gentle. Because he belongs to the Lord, his manner toward opponents must reflect his Master's own patient kindness.

basic.context: Paul warns Timothy against the pointless word-fights that were damaging the church and calls him instead to handle Scripture rightly, as a workman God approves. False teaching, like the claim that the resurrection had already happened, was spreading like an infection. Yet God's foundation is secure. Paul urges Timothy to keep himself clean, run from harmful desires, and correct those who oppose the truth gently, hoping God will bring them to repentance.
basic.hermeneutics: The central command is to handle the word of truth accurately, which sets a standard for all faithful Bible teaching: cut a straight path, do not twist it. The passage also models how to deal with error and with those caught in it, firmly rejecting the falsehood while treating people with patient kindness.
basic.originalLanguages: Timothy is to be dokimos, approved after testing, and to orthotomeo, to cut straight, the word of truth. False talk spreads like gangraina, gangrene. In the great house some are a skeuos, a vessel, for honor. And the Lord's doulos, servant or slave, must be gentle, correcting opponents in hope of their repentance.
basic.typology: The great house with its vessels pictures the church, a mixed community now, sorted at last into honorable and common use. God's sealed foundation recalls the temple built on a sure cornerstone, fulfilled in Christ.
basic.christ: The word of truth Timothy handles is the gospel of Christ, and the resurrection the false teachers distorted is the hope secured by Christ's own rising. God's firm foundation with its double seal rests on the Lord who knows His own and calls them to holiness.
basic.god: God approves genuine workers, knows those who are truly His, and holds His foundation firm no matter how error spreads. He is the Master served by honorable vessels, and He alone grants repentance that leads people out of the devil's trap.
basic.crossReferences: First Timothy 1 also names Hymenaeus among those whose faith was shipwrecked. First Corinthians 15 defends the future bodily resurrection the false teachers denied. Numbers 16 stands behind the seal, the Lord knows those who are His.
basic.oneStory: God is building a people on a foundation that cannot be shaken, sealed as His own and called to holiness. Error will spread and some will fall away, but the Lord knows His own, and He uses cleansed, gentle servants to reclaim the wandering, moving His story toward a purified people fit for the Master's use.

context.literary: The movement is framed by warnings against quarreling over words, at the start and near the end, which brackets the whole unit. In between Paul sets a series of contrasts: the approved workman against the empty talker, the spreading gangrene against the standing foundation, vessels for honor against vessels for dishonor, and the quarrelsome disputant against the gentle servant. The imagery shifts from craftsmanship to medicine to household to slavery.
context.historical: Specific false teachers, Hymenaeus and Philetus, were teaching that the resurrection had already occurred, probably an over-spiritualized view that denied a future bodily resurrection and unsettled believers. Such teaching, along with speculative word-battles, was corroding the Ephesian church, and Paul writes to arm Timothy against it.
context.canonical: Verse 15 has become a landmark text on faithful biblical interpretation, the workman who rightly handles the word. Verse 19, with its double seal, ties the security of God's people to His electing knowledge and to the call to holiness, themes that run across Scripture.

hermeneutics.authorIntent: To call Timothy to handle Scripture accurately and to shun corrosive controversy, to reassure him that God's foundation stands despite false teachers, and to shape his character and manner so that he is a clean vessel and a gentle servant who might win opponents to repentance.
hermeneutics.descriptionVsPrescription: The report about Hymenaeus and Philetus is descriptive, a real case of error. The commands to handle the word rightly, avoid empty chatter, cleanse oneself, flee youthful passions, and correct opponents gently are prescriptive, binding on every servant of the Lord.

debates.question: What does it mean to rightly handle, or cut straight, the word of truth?

views.label: To interpret and teach Scripture accurately
views.case: The workman keeps a straight course through the text, teaching its true sense rather than twisting it, the plain sense of cutting straight applied to Scripture.

views.label: To live and minister a straight, undistorted path
views.case: The image of cutting a straight road may also touch conduct, the worker whose whole ministry runs true, though the immediate object is the word of truth.

debates.bearing: The command sets accuracy and integrity as the standard for handling God's word, over against the crooked, ruinous talk Paul condemns.

debates.question: Who are the vessels for honor and dishonor in the great house?

views.label: True and false teachers within the visible church
views.case: The context of Hymenaeus and Philetus suggests the vessels for common use are the corrupting teachers, and the honorable ones are the faithful.

views.label: A call to every believer to be cleansed for honorable use
views.case: Verse 21 turns personal: anyone who cleanses himself becomes a vessel for honor, so the picture presses each person to holiness and usefulness.

debates.bearing: Both readings drive the same application: separate from what is unfit and be made useful to the Master, sanctified and ready for every good work.

study.originalLanguages: Two craftsman words anchor the movement. Timothy is to be dokimos, approved after testing, presenting himself to God as metal proved genuine, in deliberate contrast to the false teachers later called adokimos, failed the test. And he is to orthotomeo, to cut straight, the word of truth, driving a straight furrow or road through Scripture rather than twisting its path. Against this, false talk is gangraina, gangrene, spreading rot that must be cut off. The household image gives skeuos, vessel: cleansed from what is unfit, a person becomes a vessel for honor, useful to the Master. Finally the Lord's doulos, His slave, is marked not by debating skill but by kindness, patience, and gentleness, correcting opponents in the hope that God grants repentance.

typology.type: God's firm foundation with its double seal
typology.fulfillment: The church built on Christ the sure cornerstone, marked as God's own and called to holiness, echoing temple and covenant imagery.

typology.type: The great house and its vessels
typology.fulfillment: The mixed community of the church now, sorted finally into honorable and common use, a call to be cleansed for the Master's service.

study.christ: The word of truth that Timothy must handle straight is the gospel of Jesus Christ, and the resurrection distorted by Hymenaeus and Philetus is the very hope anchored in Christ's own rising and the bodily resurrection to come. God's firm foundation, sealed with the Lord knows those who are His, rests on Christ the sure cornerstone who knows and keeps His people. The gentle servant who hopes for opponents' repentance mirrors the patient kindness of the Lord he serves, the One who leads sinners to a knowledge of the truth.
study.god: God is the tester who approves genuine workers and the Master served by honorable vessels. His foundation stands unmoved even as error spreads, sealed by His sure knowledge of His own and His call to holiness. He knows those who are His, and He alone grants repentance, so that even those trapped by the devil may come to their senses and escape. Human faithfulness matters, but the security of the church rests on God's unshakable character.

crossReferences.note: Hymenaeus is named among those whose faith was shipwrecked, linking to the false teachers here.
Paul defends the reality of the resurrection the false teachers denied by claiming it had already happened.
The Lord knows those who are His, the source of the first line of the seal in verse 19.
The church built on the foundation of the apostles and prophets, with Christ the cornerstone, behind the firm foundation image.

study.oneStory: God is raising a temple-people on a foundation that cannot be shaken, marked as His own and summoned to holiness. False teaching will spread like infection and unsettle some, but the Lord knows those who are His. Through cleansed and gentle servants He recovers the wandering and grants repentance, carrying forward His work of gathering a purified people, useful to the Master and prepared for every good work.

sermon.title: Pastor and People
sermon.why: Part of Begg's series A Study in 2 Timothy, this message expounds 2 Timothy 2:14-15, the call to avoid ruinous quarrels over words and to be an approved workman who accurately handles the word of truth, setting the standard for faithful, careful handling of Scripture.

quiz.q: What does it mean to be an approved workman who accurately handles the word of truth?

options.text: To interpret and teach Scripture accurately, cutting a straight path through it.
options.note: The image is cutting straight, handling the word without twisting it.

options.text: To win as many arguments about words as possible.
options.note: Paul condemns quarreling over words as leading to ruin.

options.text: To reshape Scripture to fit what listeners want to hear.
options.note: That is the crooked handling Paul warns against, not straight cutting.

options.text: To handle God's word with care and integrity so it runs true.
options.note: True and close, though the specific image is cutting a straight path through the word.

quiz.q: How does Paul describe the effect of empty, godless chatter?

options.text: It spreads like gangrene, corroding faith wherever it goes.
options.note: Verse 17 uses the medical image of spreading rot.

options.text: It is harmless talk that does no lasting damage.
options.note: Paul treats it as a deadly, spreading infection.

options.text: It leads only to more ungodliness.
options.note: True from verse 16, though the vivid picture is the spreading gangrene.

options.text: It is a minor issue best simply ignored.
options.note: Paul takes it seriously and names offenders; it must be avoided, not shrugged off.

quiz.q: What double seal marks God's firm foundation in verse 19?

options.text: The Lord knows those who are His, and His people must turn from iniquity.
options.note: The seal joins God's electing knowledge with the call to holiness.

options.text: That the foundation will eventually crumble under false teaching.
options.note: The point is the opposite: it stands firm despite error.

options.text: That God knows and keeps His own people securely.
options.note: True, the first line of the seal, but the second line about turning from iniquity is also part of it.

options.text: That only the most gifted teachers truly belong to God.
options.note: Belonging rests on God knowing His own, not on giftedness.

quiz.q: How does one become a vessel for honorable use in the great house?

options.text: By cleansing oneself from what is unfit, becoming useful to the Master.
options.note: Verse 21 ties honorable use to being cleansed and sanctified.

options.text: By being made of gold or silver rather than wood or clay.
options.note: The material is the picture; the real issue is being cleansed for honorable use.

options.text: By nothing at all; usefulness is fixed and cannot change.
options.note: Paul says anyone who cleanses himself becomes a vessel for honor.

options.text: By separating from what defiles and pursuing holiness.
options.note: True and near, though the verse frames it as cleansing oneself to be useful to the Master.

quiz.q: How is the Lord's servant to treat those who oppose the truth?

options.text: Kindly and gently, correcting them in hope God grants repentance.
options.note: Verses 24 to 25 call for kindness, patience, and gentle correction.

options.text: With sharp quarreling until he wins the argument.
options.note: Paul says the servant must not be quarrelsome.

options.text: By ignoring them completely and never engaging.
options.note: He is to correct them, but gently, not to ignore them.

options.text: Patiently and able to teach, leaving room for God to work.
options.note: True and close, capturing the manner, with the aim being repentance and knowledge of the truth.

questions: Paul prizes accurate handling of Scripture over clever debate. How carefully do you handle the Bible, and where might you be tempted to bend it to suit yourself or win a point?
Empty, godless talk spreads like gangrene. What conversations or content in your life are quietly corroding your faith, and what would it look like to cut them off?
God's foundation stands, sealed by the truth that the Lord knows those who are His. How does resting in God knowing you steady you when error and confusion are all around?
A vessel for honor is one that has been cleansed. What in your life is God asking you to be cleansed from so you can be more useful to the Master?
The Lord's servant corrects opponents with gentleness, hoping for their repentance. Who in your life is opposing the truth, and how might patient kindness serve them better than winning an argument?

translationNotes.note: Some manuscripts read the Lord rather than God in the phrase charging them before God, as the BSB footnote observes; the sense is unchanged.
Accurately handles renders a verb meaning to cut straight, the image of a straight furrow or road driven through the word of truth.
The first line of the seal, the Lord knows those who are His, draws on Numbers 16:5, as the BSB footnote notes; see also the Greek Old Testament.
Cleanses himself of what is unfit is literally cleanses himself of these, referring back to the dishonorable vessels and their corrupting talk, per the BSB footnote.

meta.notes: BSB text fetched from bible.helloao.org for 2 Timothy 2:14-26. Curly quotation marks within verse 19 are from the source text. No em dashes present in this movement's text.
